metadata.working_title: Strong Positive Selection Biases Identity-By-Descent-Based Inferences of Recent Demography and Population Structure in Plasmodium falciparum

abstract_sketch.problem: IBD-based genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum is used to estimate parasite relatedness, effective population size (Ne), and population structure.
abstract_sketch.gap: Strong positive selection from antimalarial drug resistance may distort IBD distributions and bias demographic inferences, but the extent of this bias and how to correct it have not been systematically characterized.
abstract_sketch.approach: We combine population-genetic simulations with a true-IBD inference algorithm (tskibd) and whole-genome sequence data from 640 new plus 4,026 publicly available P. falciparum clinical isolates to quantify selection bias and test an IBD peak removal correction.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Positive selection (s = 0.3) underestimates Ne and blurs population structure; removing IBD peak regions restores accuracy in low-background-relatedness populations (West Africa) but does not materially change inferences in high-background-relatedness populations (Southeast Asia).
abstract_sketch.impact: We provide evidence-based guidance for when selection correction is needed in malaria genomic surveillance and a benchmarking framework for IBD methods in high-recombination species.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Malaria remains a global health burden, and genomic surveillance is a key tool for tracking transmission and drug resistance.
Identity-by-descent (IBD) segments summarize recent shared ancestry and are widely used to infer relatedness, Ne, and population structure in P. falciparum.
Existing IBD-based estimators assume neutrality, but P. falciparum genomes show strong recent selection from antimalarial drugs (artemisinin, chloroquine, piperaquine resistance).
Prior work on LD-based Ne estimation has reached conflicting conclusions about selection sensitivity, and no systematic evaluation exists for IBD-based inferences in Pf.
We ask: does strong positive selection bias IBD-based Ne and structure inference, and can peak-region removal correct it, and does the correction depend on background relatedness?

sections.title: Related Work
content_bullets: IBD inference methods for P. falciparum: hmmIBD and isoRelate as HMM-based callers on haploid genomes.
IBD-based Ne estimators (IBDNe) and LD-based alternatives.
IBD-based selection detection (XiR,s and related statistics).
Simulation frameworks for tree-sequence genealogies (msprime, SLiM, tskit).
Empirical surveys of P. falciparum structure and drug resistance in SEA and WAF.

subsections.title: Parasite isolates and WGS data summary
content_bullets: 2,055 analyzable Pf isolates from eastern SEA across 14 years and 18 provinces in Cambodia, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam.
751 (640 new) in-house and 1,304 public MalariaGEN Pf6 isolates.
79.3%, 68.0%, and 46.1% had >=5x, 10x, 25x coverage in >80% of genome.
80% monoclonal by Fws > 0.95; 44.3% of polyclonal had a predominant clone.
WAF validation: 50.7% monoclonal.

subsections.title: Effects of positive selection on IBD distribution and Ne
content_bullets: True-IBD via tskibd from tree sequences circumvents IBD-call quality.
Selection (s = 0.3, starting 80 generations ago, single origin at 33.3 cM) increases proportion of long IBD and pairwise total IBD, and enriches IBD near selected sites.
IBDNe underestimates Ne under selection; removing IBD peaks restores Ne toward the neutral/truth.
Short segments (0.2-2 cM) cover >25 generations; signed-rank tests across replicates show Bonferroni-adjusted p < 0.05.

subsections.title: Effects of positive selection on population structure
content_bullets: One-dimensional stepping-stone model with five subpopulations and migration rate 0.01.
Selection inflates inter-population IBD and reduces network modularity, collapsing InfoMap communities.
Peak removal restores modularity and recovers true population labels.

subsections.title: Genome-wide IBD and selection signals in SEA
content_bullets: hmmIBD-based IBD coverage shows peaks at known resistance loci: pfmdr1, pfaat1, pfcrt, dhps, pph, gch1, kelch13, arps10, ap2-g/ap2-g2.
After removing highly related isolates, n = 701 unrelated isolates with a 5x lower baseline IBD.

subsections.title: IBD-based Ne and structure in SEA (low transmission, high relatedness)
content_bullets: Ne decreases from ~10^4 to ~10^3 over recent 60-80 generations.
InfoMap finds five communities; C0 enriched in Western Cambodia; C1 spans Northeastern Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam.
Community-specific drug-resistance allele frequencies (Kelch13 C493H/R539T/C580Y, PfCRT, DHFR, DHPS).
Peak removal does not materially change Ne or structure (confidence intervals overlap).

subsections.title: Peak removal in a high-transmission (WAF) setting
content_bullets: In WAF, peak removal raises recent-generation Ne with non-overlapping CIs around 20 generations ago.
Before correction, most isolates collapse into a dominant community; after correction, finer community structure emerges.
Jackknife resampling confirms the structure difference is statistically significant.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Para 1: Summarize main finding — selection distorts IBD-based Ne and structure; peak removal helps in low-relatedness settings.
Para 2: Mechanism — longer IBD in selected regions inflates pairwise total IBD and violates TMRCA-length assumption.
Para 3: Background relatedness as modifier — when baseline IBD is high (SEA), local excess from selection is dominated by genome-wide relatedness; correction is unnecessary or over-corrects.
Para 4: Comparison with prior LD-based Ne work (Novo et al.); different conclusions driven by strength/locus/timing of selection.
Para 5: Methodological implications — benchmarking framework for IBD callers in high-recombination species, need for quantitative criteria for when to correct.
Para 6: Limitations — parameter assumptions, multi-locus simultaneous selection approximation, heterogeneous sampling.
Para 7: Practical guidance for genomic surveillance.
